items.question_text: Complete the note's rationale: Traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collision between aircraft operating within Class B, Class C, or Class D surface areas and the terminal radar service areas, and _____ aircraft operating in proximity to terminal locations.
items.explanation: The note extends the benefit to transiting aircraft near terminal locations, not just aircraft inside surface areas.

choices.text: transiting

items.question_text: A controller at a tower is providing service to a VFR aircraft. The controller has personally observed three aircraft in the pattern but receives a fourth call from an aircraft whose exact location has not yet been determined. According to 3-1-1, what is the correct basis for controlling the known traffic?
items.explanation: 3-1-1 says service is based 'only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions.' The controller must work from what is actually observed or known, applying known information to the known aircraft and not filling gaps with assumptions for the unknown one.

choices.text: Provide service to all four based on the controller's judgment about the most probable scenario.
Assume the fourth aircraft is following the published pattern and integrate it based on that assumption.
Control the three observed aircraft based on the observed positions and airport conditions, and handle the fourth only when its position is known.
Withhold all service until all four aircraft have been positively identified on radar.

items.question_text: What is the operational reason the note gives for why traffic information is especially valuable around terminal locations?
items.explanation: The note states traffic information can aid pilots 'due to the limited space around terminal locations.' This is the explicit rationale for why the aid is needed.

choices.text: The regulatory requirement that all terminal traffic be positively separated.
The higher density of instrument-rated pilots at towered airports.
The absence of radar coverage below 2,000 feet at most terminals.
The limited space around terminal locations.

items.question_text: Which of the following best characterizes the relationship between ATC traffic information and the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility under the 3-1-1 note?
items.explanation: The note says traffic information 'can aid' pilots — it is helpful but does not replace or share the pilot's responsibility. The pilot 'is responsible' regardless of whether ATC provides traffic information.

choices.text: Traffic information makes ATC solely responsible for collision avoidance once it is issued.
Traffic information aids pilots but does not shift or share the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility.
Traffic information and pilot responsibility are mutually exclusive — only one can apply at a time.
Traffic information creates a shared legal duty between the controller and the pilot.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology template: "(Item) _____ (observed condition)."
items.explanation: The phraseology format uses 'APPEAR/S' because the word 'appears' conveys that the controller is reporting what is observed, not making a definitive mechanical diagnosis.

choices.text: APPEARS

items.question_text: Why does the phraseology use 'appears' rather than a definitive statement like 'your landing gear is up'?
items.explanation: The word 'appears' acknowledges that the controller is reporting a visual observation, not a mechanical determination. The pilot is the one who can verify instruments and system status.

choices.text: Because FAA regulations prohibit controllers from using any descriptive language about aircraft parts.
Because definitive statements about aircraft condition are reserved for maintenance personnel only.
Because the controller is reporting a visual observation, not certifying a mechanical condition.
Because 'appears' is less likely to startle the pilot than a direct assertion.

items.question_text: A controller sees smoke trailing from an aircraft's engine nacelle. The pilot has not requested any information. Under 3-1-10, what determines whether the controller should advise the pilot?
items.explanation: The paragraph says to inform the aircraft 'when you deem it necessary' — the controller's judgment about the observed abnormal condition triggers the advisory, even without a pilot request.

choices.text: Approval from the facility supervisor before passing any observation.
Only a specific pilot request for an abnormality check.
Confirmation of the abnormality from at least one other aircraft on frequency.
The controller's judgment that the observed abnormal condition makes an advisory necessary.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-11, what is the prerequisite condition for approving a pilot's request to cross a Class C or D surface area or exceed the surface-area speed limit?
items.explanation: The paragraph begins with 'If traffic conditions permit' as the threshold condition for both crossing and speed-limit approval.

choices.text: traffic conditions permit

items.question_text: A pilot requests to cross a Class D surface area at 220 knots. Traffic conditions permit. The controller is about to approve the request when the pilot adds that they'd also like to speed up to 275 knots because the flight manual recommends it. Under 3-1-11, what should the controller do?
items.explanation: The paragraph allows exceeding the surface-area speed limit subject to the 250-knot cap unless the pilot informs the controller that a higher minimum speed is required. The pilot has now stated that the flight manual recommends the higher speed, which falls under the note's allowance. Since the pilot has informed the controller of the requirement, the 250-knot cap exception applies.

choices.text: Deny the crossing entirely because speeds above 250 knots are never allowed in surface areas.
Approve only 250 knots because the note does not mention flight manual recommendations as a valid exception.
Approve 275 knots because the pilot has stated the higher speed is required by the flight manual, which satisfies the exception.
Approve 220 knots only and instruct the pilot to accelerate after leaving the surface area.

items.question_text: Which of the following is NOT listed in the 3-1-11 note as an unusual maneuver?
items.explanation: The note lists unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, practice instrument approaches to altitudes below specified minima (unless a landing or touch-and-go is to be made), and buzz jobs. Normal touch-and-goes are not unusual maneuvers.

choices.text: A buzz job at low altitude and high speed.
An unnecessary low pass over the field.
An unscheduled flyby.
A touch-and-go landing conducted under tower instruction.

items.question_text: What two things does the note say are increased by unusual maneuvers?
items.explanation: The note concludes that such maneuvers 'increase hazards to persons and property and contribute to noise complaints.'

choices.text: Fuel consumption and airframe stress.
Hazards to persons and property, and noise complaints.
Controller workload and frequency congestion.
Runway occupancy time and separation minima.

items.question_text: Complete: Local controllers must visually scan runways to the _____ extent possible.
items.explanation: The paragraph uses 'maximum extent possible' to establish that runway scanning is the priority duty for local controllers.

choices.text: maximum

items.question_text: A tower has runways that are widely separated with clear sight lines and no movement areas between them. Under 3-1-12, how does the ground controller's scanning obligation differ from a tower where runways are very close to active taxiways?
items.explanation: Ground must always assist, but the paragraph singles out 'especially when runways are in close proximity to other movement areas' as the situation where the assisting role is most critical. Both situations require assistance, but the emphasis varies.

choices.text: Ground's scanning obligation is identical regardless of the runway layout or proximity to movement areas.
Ground must assist only when runways are close to other movement areas; otherwise ground has no scanning duty.
Ground must assist in both cases, but the obligation is especially emphasized when runways are close to other movement areas.
With widely separated runways, ground must take over primary scanning duty from local.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology: "(A/c call sign) REMAIN OUTSIDE _____ AIRSPACE AND STANDBY."
items.explanation: The phraseology specifically says 'DELTA' airspace because this rule applies to Class D airspace.

choices.text: DELTA

items.question_text: A pilot calls the tower on initial contact and the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' The controller is then distracted by an emergency and cannot provide services for several minutes. The pilot, having heard 'standby,' enters the Class D. Under 3-1-13, is the pilot's entry permitted?
items.explanation: The paragraph says that when the controller responds with the call sign and 'standby,' communications are established and the pilot CAN enter. However, if workload prevents immediate service provision, the controller must tell the pilot to REMAIN OUTSIDE. In this scenario, the controller said only 'standby' — which does establish communications and legally permits entry — but the controller should have used the 'remain outside' instruction to keep the pilot out. Since the controller only said 'standby,' the pilot's entry was technically permitted.

choices.text: No, because any gap in controller attention automatically revokes the prior communication establishment.
No, because the controller intended to delay services, which implicitly means the pilot must remain outside.
Yes, because 'standby' with the call sign established two-way communications, which is all that is required for Class D entry.
Yes, but only if the pilot first requests and receives an explicit 'enter the Delta' clearance.

items.question_text: What is the critical operational difference between responding to an initial Class D call with '(call sign) standby' versus '(call sign) REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY'?
items.explanation: Both establish communications. The difference is that in the second case, the controller adds the explicit instruction keeping the aircraft outside, recognizing that services cannot yet be provided. The first allows entry; the second prevents it.

choices.text: There is no operational difference — both mean the pilot must wait outside.
'Standby' alone does not establish communications; only the longer phraseology establishes communications.
The first response denies entry; the second permits entry after a delay.
In both cases communications are established, but the second adds an instruction that keeps the aircraft outside until services can be provided.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-14, both the taxi restriction and the rolling-takeoff requirement are qualified by what phrase?
items.explanation: The paragraph qualifies both measures with 'and to the extent possible,' acknowledging that operational realities may sometimes prevent perfect compliance.

choices.text: to the extent possible

items.question_text: According to the 3-1-14 note, what two operational harms does newly airborne volcanic ash cause to following aircraft?
items.explanation: The note says the newly airborne ash will 'significantly reduce visibility and will be ingested by the engines of following aircraft.'

choices.text: Brake degradation and tire wear.
Radio interference and pitot-static blockage.
Reduced visibility and engine ingestion.
Navigation system errors and compass deviation.

items.question_text: A departing aircraft at an ash-contaminated airport is holding in position on the runway. The controller normally uses a 'cleared for takeoff' call that involves a brief pause after the aircraft lines up. Under 3-1-14, what should the controller do differently?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers to 'provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures' to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present, because stopping and restarting on ash surfaces makes large amounts of ash airborne again.

choices.text: Use standard procedures — volcanic ash on the surface does not change takeoff clearance procedures.
Require the aircraft to hold in position longer than usual to verify ash clearance before takeoff.
Cancel all departures until the ash has been completely removed from the runway surface.
Issue the takeoff clearance so the aircraft can begin its roll without stopping, to the extent possible.

items.question_text: Complete the unable phraseology: "(Identification) UNABLE DUE TO _____ DISRUPTION."
items.explanation: The prescribed unable phraseology under 3-1-15 ends with 'OPERATIONAL DISRUPTION.'

choices.text: OPERATIONAL

items.question_text: A PIC requests to return to the gate under the Tarmac Rule. The controller issues 'EXPECT A 15 MINUTE DELAY DUE TO DEPARTING TRAFFIC.' The pilot accepts the delay but calls back 20 minutes later still waiting. Under 3-1-15, what response options does the controller now have?
items.explanation: The paragraph's two-part response applies on each request: provide service as soon as operationally practical, or advise of significant disruption. The controller may assess whether conditions have changed since the DELAY advisory and apply either response again. The DELAY phraseology is one of three options the controller can use.

choices.text: Once a delay advisory is issued, the controller may not switch to an unable response for the same request.
The expect delay advisory commits the controller to providing service after the stated time without further assessment.
Re-evaluate whether to provide the return service or advise of significant disruption — the expect delay advisory is just one of the available response options.
The pilot must make a new request under different wording to receive a different response.

items.question_text: According to the 3-1-15 note, where are the specific actions that constitute a 'significant disruption' defined?
items.explanation: The note says facility procedures 'must be identified in the facility directive pertaining to the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule' and that they 'vary by airport.'

choices.text: In each airline's operating certificate, filed with the airport authority.
In the DOT Rule, 14 CFR Part 259, directly.
In FAA Order JO 7110.65, as a universal definition applicable to all facilities.
In the facility directive, and they vary by airport.

items.question_text: Complete: Preventive control differs from other airport traffic control in that repetitious, routine _____ of pilot action is eliminated.
items.explanation: The note specifically says 'repetitious, routine approval of pilot action is eliminated' — that is the defining operational difference from normal tower control.

choices.text: approval

items.question_text: An aircraft not operating under any letter of agreement requests that the tower use 'preventive control' to reduce radio calls during a long pattern session. Under 3-1-2, is this permissible?
items.explanation: The paragraph says 'Provide preventive control service only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement' (or DoD equivalents per the note). It is not available by pilot request alone.

choices.text: Yes — but only during VFR conditions and when the tower is not busy.
Yes — any pilot can request preventive control to reduce frequency congestion.
No — preventive control service requires an LOA or its DoD equivalent; it cannot be provided on pilot request alone.
No — unless the pilot also files a written request with the facility at least 24 hours in advance.

items.question_text: Under preventive control, when do controllers intervene?
items.explanation: The note says 'Controllers intervene only when they observe a traffic conflict developing.' This is the same concept as the body's 'only if a situation develops which requires corrective action.'

choices.text: Only after the pilot has missed a scheduled position report.
At regular intervals to confirm the aircraft is still under preventive control.
Whenever another aircraft enters the movement area, regardless of conflict.
Only when they observe a traffic conflict developing.

items.question_text: Complete: Ground control must obtain approval from local control before authorizing an aircraft or a vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway. The coordination must include the _____ at the runway where the operation will occur.
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-3a requires the coordination to include 'the point/intersection at the runway where the operation will occur.'

choices.text: point or intersection

items.question_text: Ground control coordinates with local control for an aircraft to cross Runway 27 at Taxiway A. The aircraft completes the crossing. Under 3-1-3c, what must ground control now do?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-3c says 'The ground controller must advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete.' This can be verbal or through visual aids per facility directive.

choices.text: Issue the aircraft a new taxi clearance before advising local of anything.
No further action is needed — the crossing authorization automatically expires when the aircraft clears the runway.
Advise local control that the coordinated runway operation is complete, verbally or via visual aids as specified in the facility directive.
Wait for the aircraft to call clear of the runway before notifying local control.

items.question_text: Local control wants to begin using Runway 18, which has not been previously designated as active. Under 3-1-3e, what must local do first?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-3e states 'The local controller must coordinate with the ground controller before using a runway not previously designated as active.'

choices.text: No coordination is needed — local has primary responsibility for all active runways.
Announce the runway change on the ATIS and wait two minutes.
Verify that the runway has been swept within the last hour.
Coordinate with the ground controller before using the runway.

items.question_text: According to 3-1-3, under what circumstances may exceptions to positive coordination and control of active runways be authorized?
items.explanation: The note says exceptions 'may be authorized only as provided in paragraph 1-1-11, Waivers to This Order, and FAA Order JO 7210.3...paragraph 10-1-7, Use of Active Runways, where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations.'

choices.text: Whenever the facility manager determines that conditions warrant a deviation.
Only as provided in paragraph 1-1-11 and JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7, where justified by extraordinary circumstances.
During periods of light traffic when no conflicts are expected.
There are no exceptions — positive coordination is always required without exception.

items.question_text: Complete: Local and ground controllers must exchange information as necessary for the safe and efficient use of airport runways and movement areas. As a minimum, provide aircraft _____ and applicable runway/intersection/taxiway information.
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-4 says the minimum exchange must include 'aircraft identification' along with runway/intersection/taxiway information.

choices.text: identification

items.question_text: Under 3-1-4b, when must ground control notify local control about an aircraft taxied to an intersection?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-4b requires notification 'of any aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff.' This is specifically tied to intersection departures, which have wake turbulence separation implications.

choices.text: Only for heavy aircraft regardless of intersection use.
For any aircraft taxiing anywhere on the movement area.
For any aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff.
Only when the intersection departure will be followed by another departure.

items.question_text: When the runways in use are not visible from the tower or the aircraft using them are not visible on radar, what must happen under 3-1-4c before releasing an aircraft to the other controller?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-4c requires that the controller 'advise the local/ground controller of the aircraft's location before releasing the aircraft to the other controller' when runways or aircraft are not visible.

choices.text: Hold the aircraft until visual contact is regained before releasing it.
Request a position report from the pilot first.
Transfer communications only after the aircraft reports clear of the runway.
Advise the other controller of the aircraft's location.

items.question_text: Complete: When established in a letter of agreement, vehicles, equipment, and personnel in two-way communications with ATC may be authorized to operate in the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface, which includes when aircraft are _____, _____, or _____ along the runway.
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-5a states vehicles/personnel may operate in the RSA 'when aircraft are arriving, departing, or taxiing along the runway' — these are the three operational conditions listed.

choices.text: arriving departing taxiing

items.question_text: Under 3-1-5, 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' is approved phraseology for which of the following?
items.explanation: The note states 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED is not approved phraseology for instructing aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel to cross or operate on a runway.' It is used for RSA operations up to the runway edge under an LOA, not for runway crossings.

choices.text: Authorizing any vehicle to operate on the runway surface.
Instructing an aircraft to cross an active runway.
Authorizing LOA-covered vehicles to operate in the RSA up to the runway edge.
Instructing personnel to cross the runway at an intersection.

items.question_text: An airport vehicle with an LOA and two-way communications requests to operate in the RSA near Runway 18 while aircraft are arriving. Under 3-1-5a, what is the controller's correct response?
items.explanation: The paragraph authorizes LOA-covered vehicles with two-way communications to operate in the RSA 'up to the edge of the runway surface, which includes when aircraft are arriving, departing, or taxiing along the runway.' The controller may use 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' with additional instructions as needed.

choices.text: Authorize the operation only after coordinating with approach control for each arrival.
Deny the request because RSA operations are never permitted while aircraft are arriving.
Tell the vehicle to wait until all arriving aircraft have landed and cleared the runway.
Authorize the operation using 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' with any necessary additional instructions.

items.question_text: Which transmission best follows the paragraph's guidance for describing a mower to the left of Runway 27?
items.explanation: The paragraph's first example is exactly 'Mower left of runway two seven.' The guidance is to describe vehicles or personnel in a manner that helps pilots recognize them.

choices.text: "Ground equipment operating adjacent to the active runway environment."
"Mower left of runway two seven."
"Vehicle on the airfield, use caution."
"Attention all aircraft, maintenance activity in progress."

items.question_text: What is the key operational distinction between 3-1-6 subparagraph a (describing vehicles/equipment/personnel) and subparagraph b (describing relative position of traffic)?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a covers non-aircraft (vehicles, equipment, personnel) on or near the movement area that pilots need to recognize. Subparagraph b covers the relative position of other aircraft (traffic) in relation to the receiving aircraft, using easy-to-understand terms like 'to your right' or 'ahead of you.'

choices.text: Subparagraph a requires radar; subparagraph b requires visual observation.
Subparagraph a is for ground operations; subparagraph b is for airborne operations only.
Subparagraph a is for non-aircraft hazards; subparagraph b is for describing other aircraft positions relative to the receiving aircraft.
There is no distinction — both cover the same types of information.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-6c, when using a CTRD, you may issue traffic advisories using the standard radar phraseology prescribed in which paragraph?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-6c refers to paragraph 2-1-21 for standard radar traffic advisory phraseology when using a CTRD.

items.question_text: Complete: Determine the position of an aircraft, personnel or equipment before issuing taxi instructions, takeoff clearance, or authorizing personnel, and/or equipment to proceed onto the _____.
items.explanation: The paragraph requires position determination before authorizing anyone to 'proceed onto the movement area.'

choices.text: movement area

items.question_text: According to the 3-1-7 note, what are the three methods for determining the position of aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel?
items.explanation: The note says positions may be determined 'visually or through use of a display system' and 'When ATC is unable to determine position visually or via a display system, position reports may be used.'

choices.text: Via display system, through ATIS broadcasts, or by facility directive.
Visually, via radar, or through pilot certifications.
Visually, through a display system, or via position reports.
Visually, by flight plan cross-reference, or through ground vehicle reports.

items.question_text: A controller needs to issue a taxi instruction to an aircraft but cannot see the aircraft visually and the display system is not showing its position. Under 3-1-7, what is the correct course of action?
items.explanation: The note says 'When ATC is unable to determine position visually or via a display system, position reports may be used.' The controller should use a position report from the pilot before issuing instructions.

choices.text: Issue a generic 'taxi to the ramp' instruction without confirming position.
Issue the taxi instruction based on the aircraft's last known position and the expected taxi route.
Delay the taxi instruction indefinitely until the aircraft becomes visible.
Use a position report from the aircraft to determine its location before issuing the taxi instruction.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8a, for how long must a wind shear/microburst statement be included on the ATIS following the last report or indication?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-8a specifies the ATIS statement must be included 'for 20 minutes following the last report or indication of the wind shear/microburst.'

choices.text: 20 minutes

items.question_text: According to the LLWAS note in 3-1-8, what is the limitation of the LLWAS detection capability?
items.explanation: The note states 'The LLWAS is designed to detect low level wind shear conditions around the periphery of an airport. It does not detect wind shear beyond that limitation.'

choices.text: It detects wind shear only within 3 miles of the airport reference point.
It detects wind shear at all altitudes but only within Class D surface areas.
It detects wind shear only around the airport periphery and does not detect wind shear beyond that.
It detects wind shear anywhere within 50 nautical miles of the airport.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8c(2)(c), LLWAS NE++ or LLWAS−RS may detect a possible wind shear/microburst at the edge of the system but may be unable to distinguish between the two. What phraseology must accompany the alert information in this case?
items.explanation: The phraseology for this situation is '(Appropriate wind or alert information) POSSIBLE WIND SHEAR OUTSIDE THE NETWORK.'

choices.text: "MICROBURST POSSIBLE — VERIFY WITH TDWR."
"WIND SHEAR ALERT — SYSTEM EDGE UNCERTAINTY."
"UNDETERMINED ALERT AT SYSTEM PERIPHERY."
"POSSIBLE WIND SHEAR OUTSIDE THE NETWORK."

items.question_text: In 3-1-8c(2)(e), when are the LLWAS NE++ and LLWAS−RS for a specific runway arrival/departure considered out of service?
items.explanation: The paragraph states 'When all three remote sensors designated for a specific runway arrival or departure wind display line are inoperative then the LLWAS NE++ and LLWAS−RS for that runway arrival/departure must be considered out of service.' The system can operate with up to 50% of total sensors inoperative, but when all three for a specific runway line fail, that runway's service is lost.

choices.text: When any single sensor in the network fails.
When all three remote sensors designated for that runway's arrival or departure wind display line are inoperative.
When the geographic situation display is unavailable.
When 25% or more of total sensors are inoperative.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8d(3), when does the controller's responsibility for providing approved separation resume after an aircraft has initiated a wind shear escape maneuver? (Select the correct condition for departures.)
items.explanation: For departures, responsibility resumes when EITHER: (1) crew informs ATC escape is complete AND ATC observes approved separation re-established, OR (2) crew informs ATC escape is complete and has resumed previously assigned clearance. Both conditions can trigger resumption.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft has returned to its assigned altitude and heading.
Immediately upon the crew reporting the escape maneuver has begun.
When the crew informs ATC the escape is complete and either ATC observes approved separation re-established or the crew has resumed the previously assigned departure clearance.
When 20 minutes have elapsed since the last wind shear report.

items.question_text: Complete: Uncertified tower display workstations must be used only as an aid to assist controllers in visually locating aircraft or in determining their spatial relationship to known _____.
items.explanation: The paragraph says uncertified TDWs help controllers determine spatial relationship 'to known geographical points.'

choices.text: geographical points

items.question_text: Local controllers at nonapproach control towers must devote the majority of their time to what, according to the 3-1-9 note?
items.explanation: The note states 'local controllers at nonapproach control towers must devote the majority of their time to visually scanning the runways and local area.'

choices.text: Coordinating with approach control on inbound sequencing.
Monitoring the certified tower radar display for traffic advisories.
Visually scanning the runways and local area.
Processing flight progress strips and automation data.

items.question_text: Why should pilots not be advised of being in 'radar contact' when receiving services from a tower radar display, according to the 3-1-9 note?
items.explanation: The note explains that the requirements of paragraph 5-3-1 (Application) cannot be assured — specifically 'continued positive radar identification' would place 'distracting and operationally inefficient requirements upon the local controller.' Therefore, tower radar functions are not considered radar services.

choices.text: Because the displays are intended only for ground movement surveillance.
Because tower radar displays are not accurate enough for positive identification.
Because 'radar contact' is a term reserved for approach control facilities only.
Because tower radar functions are not considered radar services — continued positive radar identification requirements cannot be assured by local controllers.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-9d, if there is an ASR outage supporting the ASDE system and MLAT is inoperative or not present, what must tower positions responsible for aircraft on approach enable on the TDW?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-9d specifically requires enabling the 'ADS−B indicator on the tower display workstation(s)' under these conditions, with the note that the indicator only displays in Fused Display Mode.

choices.text: The ASDE-X safety logic processor.
The ADS-B indicator.
The multilateration backup system.
The approach surveillance secondary radar.

items.question_text: What are the three phraseology options a local controller may use to provide directions or suggested headings to VFR aircraft under 3-1-9b(3)?
items.explanation: The paragraph provides two phraseology formats: one using PROCEED direction-bound, and one using SUGGESTED HEADING. The note emphasizes these are suggestions/advisory in nature, not radar vectors.

choices.text: "VECTOR (direction)" or "RADAR NAVIGATE TO (point)."
"TURN (direction) HEADING (degrees)" or "FLY HEADING (degrees)."
"PROCEED (direction)-BOUND" or "SUGGESTED HEADING (degrees)."
"DIRECT TO (fix)" or "RESUME OWN NAVIGATION."

items.situation: You are working ground control. An aircraft needs to cross Runway 27 Left at Taxiway Bravo. You coordinate with local control and receive approval: 'CROSS RUNWAY 27 LEFT AT TAXIWAY BRAVO.' You issue the crossing instruction to the aircraft. The aircraft crosses and is now clear of the runway on the other side. Another ground controller says no further action is needed because the aircraft is clear.
items.para_context: Ground control must obtain local approval before authorizing a runway crossing. The ground controller must advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete, verbally or through visual aids specified in the facility directive.

choices.text: No further coordination is needed — the aircraft clearing the runway ends the operation.
Advise local control that the runway crossing operation at Taxiway Bravo is complete.
Wait for the aircraft to call you on the other side before notifying local.
Only notify local if the crossing took longer than expected.

items.explanation: 3-1-3C requires the ground controller to advise local when the coordinated runway operation is complete.

items.situation: You are working local control. Runway 18 is in use but not visible from the tower due to fog. You have an aircraft on the runway that you need to hand off to ground control. Another controller says to just transfer communications because ground can see the taxiways.
items.para_context: When the runways in use for landing/departing aircraft are not visible from the tower or the aircraft using them are not visible on radar, advise the local/ground controller of the aircraft's location before releasing the aircraft to the other controller.

choices.text: Hold the aircraft on the runway until visibility improves enough to see it from the tower.
Transfer communications immediately because ground can see all taxiways.
Advise ground control of the aircraft's location before releasing the aircraft to them.
Ask the pilot to report position to ground control directly after switching frequencies.

items.explanation: 3-1-4C requires the controller to advise the other controller of the aircraft's location before releasing when runways/aircraft are not visible.

items.situation: An aircraft on final approach to Runway 27 suddenly transmits 'Tower, United 1154, wind shear escape.' The aircraft pitches up and begins a full-power climb straight ahead, crossing the path of a departure climbing out of Runway 27. Another controller tells you to immediately issue a turn to both aircraft to restore separation.
items.para_context: If an aircraft under your control informs you that it is performing a wind shear escape, do not issue control instructions that are contrary to pilot actions. ATC should continue to provide safety alerts regarding terrain or obstacles and traffic advisories for the escape aircraft, as appropriate. Once the responding aircraft has initiated a wind shear escape maneuver, the controller is not responsible for providing approved separation between the escape aircraft and any other aircraft.

choices.text: Hand the escape aircraft off to approach control because tower cannot handle escape maneuvers.
Immediately issue turns to both aircraft to restore separation the moment the escape maneuver begins.
Direct the escape aircraft to level off immediately because it is crossing another aircraft's path.
Do not issue contrary control instructions to the escape aircraft. Provide traffic advisories and safety alerts as appropriate, and continue normal control of other aircraft.

items.explanation: 3-1-8D(1) says do not issue contrary instructions during an escape. 3-1-8d(3) says separation responsibility is suspended during the escape. The controller should provide safety alerts and traffic advisories without interfering with the escape maneuver.

items.situation: You are working local control at a tower with an uncertified tower display workstation. A VFR aircraft calls, uncertain of its position, and asks if you can see them on radar and give them a traffic advisory. Another controller says to provide standard radar traffic advisories using the uncertified display since you can see the aircraft.
items.para_context: Uncertified tower display workstations must be used only as an aid to assist controllers in visually locating aircraft or in determining their spatial relationship to known geographical points. Radar services and traffic advisories are not to be provided using uncertified tower display workstations. General information may be given in an easy to understand manner, such as 'to your right' or 'ahead of you.'

choices.text: Provide standard radar traffic advisories using the uncertified display since the aircraft is visible.
Do not provide radar traffic advisories. Give general position information in an easy-to-understand manner using geographic references, or help visually locate the aircraft.
Tell the aircraft you cannot help at all because the display is uncertified.
Transfer the aircraft to approach control because towers cannot provide any position information.

items.explanation: 3-1-9A prohibits radar services and traffic advisories using uncertified TDWs, but allows general position information using easy-to-understand references to help the pilot visually locate other aircraft.
